name: Philosophy
description: Explore ancient wisdom, ethics, logic, and humanity's deepest questions.

questions.text: Who is known as the father of Western philosophy?
questions.explanation: Socrates is widely regarded as the father of Western philosophy for his method of inquiry and influence on later thinkers.

questions.text: Who wrote 'The Republic'?
questions.explanation: Plato wrote 'The Republic,' a dialogue exploring justice, the ideal state, and the philosopher-king concept.

questions.text: What branch of philosophy deals with questions of right and wrong?
questions.explanation: Ethics is the branch of philosophy concerned with moral principles, values, and questions of right and wrong conduct.

questions.text: Who said 'I think, therefore I am'?
questions.explanation: René Descartes wrote 'Cogito, ergo sum' ('I think, therefore I am') as the foundational certainty of his philosophical method.

questions.text: What is the Socratic method?
options: Teaching through cooperative dialogue and questioning
questions.explanation: The Socratic method is a form of cooperative dialogue involving questioning to stimulate critical thinking and expose contradictions.

questions.text: Which ancient Greek philosopher tutored Alexander the Great?
questions.explanation: Aristotle was the personal tutor of Alexander the Great, teaching him from age 13.

questions.text: What is the study of knowledge and how we know things called?
questions.explanation: Epistemology is the branch of philosophy that studies the nature, origin, and scope of knowledge and belief.

questions.text: Which philosopher wrote 'Meditations on First Philosophy'?
questions.explanation: René Descartes wrote 'Meditations on First Philosophy' (1641), seeking to rebuild knowledge on certain foundations.

questions.text: What is metaphysics concerned with?
options: The fundamental nature of reality and existence
questions.explanation: Metaphysics explores fundamental questions about reality, existence, time, space, causality, and the nature of being.

questions.text: Which philosopher is associated with the concept of the 'Categorical Imperative'?
questions.explanation: Immanuel Kant developed the Categorical Imperative, the principle that one should act only according to rules one could universalize.

questions.text: Who founded the philosophical school of Stoicism?
questions.explanation: Stoicism was founded by Zeno of Citium around 300 BCE, emphasizing virtue, reason, and living in accordance with nature.

questions.text: What does 'utilitarianism' hold as the basis for moral decisions?
options: Maximizing happiness or well-being for the greatest number
questions.explanation: Utilitarianism, developed by Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill, holds that morally right actions maximize overall happiness.

questions.text: Which philosopher wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'?
questions.explanation: Friedrich Nietzsche wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra' (1883-1885), introducing concepts like the Übermensch and eternal recurrence.

questions.text: What is the philosophical concept of 'tabula rasa'?
options: The idea that the mind is blank at birth, shaped by experience
The theory that knowledge is innate
questions.explanation: Tabula rasa ('blank slate') is the theory, associated with John Locke, that the mind is empty at birth and knowledge comes from experience.

questions.text: Which ancient philosophical school taught that pleasure is the highest good?
questions.explanation: Epicureanism, founded by Epicurus, taught that pleasure (particularly tranquility and freedom from pain) is the highest good.

questions.text: Who wrote 'Being and Nothingness'?
questions.explanation: Jean-Paul Sartre wrote 'Being and Nothingness' (1943), the foundational text of existentialist philosophy.

questions.text: The phrase 'the unexamined life is not worth living' is attributed to whom?
questions.explanation: Socrates said 'The unexamined life is not worth living' at his trial, according to Plato's Apology.

questions.text: What is Plato's 'Allegory of the Cave' meant to illustrate?
options: The difference between reality and perception/ignorance
questions.explanation: Plato's Allegory of the Cave illustrates how most humans mistake appearances for reality, while philosophers seek true knowledge.

questions.text: What is 'existentialism' primarily concerned with?
options: Individual freedom, choice, and responsibility in creating meaning
questions.explanation: Existentialism focuses on individual human existence, freedom, and the responsibility to create one's own meaning in a universe without inherent purpose.

questions.text: What is the 'Ship of Theseus' paradox about?
options: The ethics of war and conquest
Whether an object that has had all its parts replaced remains the same object
The proper use of metaphor in argument
questions.explanation: The Ship of Theseus paradox asks whether an object remains the same when all its original components are gradually replaced.

questions.text: Which philosopher introduced the concept of the 'Übermensch' (Overman)?
questions.explanation: Friedrich Nietzsche introduced the Übermensch in 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra' as an ideal of a human who creates their own values.

questions.text: What is the 'trolley problem' in ethics?
options: A puzzle about transportation resource allocation
A thought experiment exploring whether to sacrifice one to save many
questions.explanation: The trolley problem is a thought experiment asking whether it is ethical to divert a threat to kill one person to save five others.

questions.text: Who developed the philosophy known as 'Pragmatism'?
questions.explanation: Pragmatism was developed primarily by Charles Sanders Peirce, William James, and John Dewey as a distinctly American philosophical tradition.

questions.text: What does the philosophical position of 'solipsism' hold?
options: Only one's own mind can be known to exist with certainty
questions.explanation: Solipsism is the view that only one's own mind is certain to exist; the external world cannot be known to be real.

questions.text: Who wrote 'Critique of Pure Reason'?
questions.explanation: Immanuel Kant published 'Critique of Pure Reason' (1781), examining the limits of human knowledge and reason.

questions.text: What is 'dialectical materialism', associated with Marx and Engels?
options: The view that ideas drive historical change
The theory that material conditions and class conflict drive historical development
The philosophy that ethics depends on context
questions.explanation: Dialectical materialism holds that the material world and class conflict between groups drive historical change and development.

questions.text: What does 'a priori' knowledge mean in philosophy?
options: Knowledge that is independent of experience or observation
questions.explanation: A priori knowledge is knowledge that can be known independently of experience, such as logical and mathematical truths.

questions.text: Which philosopher is most associated with the 'social contract' theory?
options: Thomas Hobbes, John Locke, and Rousseau
questions.explanation: Social contract theory is most associated with Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau, each offering distinct versions of how society is formed.

questions.text: What is the 'problem of evil' in philosophy of religion?
options: Whether humans are naturally good or evil
The challenge of explaining why a good God permits suffering and evil
The origin of immoral behavior in society
The philosophy of punishment and justice
questions.explanation: The problem of evil questions how an omnipotent, omniscient, and benevolent God can permit evil and suffering to exist.

questions.text: Who is associated with the philosophy of 'Phenomenology'?
questions.explanation: Edmund Husserl founded phenomenology, a method of philosophical inquiry that focuses on the structure of conscious experience.

questions.text: What is the 'Euthyphro dilemma', as posed by Socrates?
options: Whether gods can change their nature
Whether something is good because gods approve of it, or gods approve because it is good
Whether humans can know divine will
questions.explanation: The Euthyphro dilemma asks: Is something pious because gods love it, or do gods love it because it is pious? It challenges divine command theory.

questions.text: What is 'Occam's Razor'?
options: The principle that simpler explanations should be preferred over complex ones
A method for testing philosophical arguments
A theory about the nature of consciousness
questions.explanation: Occam's Razor, attributed to William of Ockham, is the principle that among competing hypotheses, the one with fewest assumptions should be preferred.

questions.text: Which philosopher wrote 'The Prince'?
questions.explanation: Niccolò Machiavelli wrote 'The Prince' (1513), a political treatise advising rulers on gaining and maintaining power.

questions.text: What is 'nihilism' in philosophy?
options: The belief that nothing has inherent meaning or value
The belief that everything happens for a reason
questions.explanation: Nihilism is the philosophical position that life lacks inherent meaning, moral truths are groundless, and values are baseless.

questions.text: In Buddhist philosophy, what is 'nirvana'?
options: A state of heavenly reward after death
Liberation from suffering and the cycle of rebirth
questions.explanation: In Buddhism, nirvana is the state of liberation from suffering, desire, and the cycle of rebirth (samsara).

questions.text: What is Hegel's concept of the 'dialectic' in its simplest form?
options: A process of thesis, antithesis, and synthesis driving historical development
The contradiction inherent in all ideas
questions.explanation: Hegel's dialectic describes how ideas (thesis) generate opposition (antithesis), which resolves into a higher synthesis, driving historical progress.

questions.text: What is the 'hard problem of consciousness' as articulated by David Chalmers?
options: How we measure the complexity of thought
Explaining why physical processes give rise to subjective experience
questions.explanation: Chalmers' hard problem asks why physical brain processes produce subjective, first-person conscious experience at all.

questions.text: What is 'epistemic humility'?
options: Recognizing the limits of one's knowledge and being open to being wrong
questions.explanation: Epistemic humility is the intellectual virtue of recognizing the limits of one's knowledge and remaining open to correction.

questions.text: Which philosopher introduced the concept of 'bad faith' (mauvaise foi)?
questions.explanation: Sartre introduced 'bad faith' to describe the self-deception of denying one's own freedom and responsibility by pretending to be determined.

questions.text: What is 'hermeneutics' in philosophy?
options: The theory and methodology of text interpretation
questions.explanation: Hermeneutics is the theory and practice of interpretation, especially of texts, originally applied to biblical exegesis.

questions.text: Which philosopher argued that 'God is dead' and explored the implications for morality?
questions.explanation: Nietzsche proclaimed 'God is dead' in 'The Gay Science,' meaning that the Enlightenment had destroyed the foundations of religious morality.

questions.text: What is the 'veil of ignorance' thought experiment by John Rawls?
options: The idea that we cannot know moral truth
A device to determine just principles by imagining we don't know our place in society
questions.explanation: Rawls' veil of ignorance asks what principles we'd choose if we didn't know our social position, wealth, or talents, ensuring impartiality.

questions.text: What is 'logical positivism'?
options: The belief that logic can solve all philosophical problems
The view that only statements verifiable by logic or science are meaningful
A positive approach to ethical questions
The claim that existence precedes essence
questions.explanation: Logical positivism holds that only statements that are empirically verifiable or analytically true are meaningful; metaphysics is nonsense.

questions.text: Who developed the philosophy of 'Deconstruction'?
questions.explanation: Jacques Derrida developed deconstruction, a method of critical analysis that exposes contradictions and instabilities in texts.

questions.text: What is Spinoza's concept of 'Deus sive Natura' (God or Nature)?
options: The idea that God created nature separately
The pantheistic view that God and Nature are one and the same substance
The argument that nature proves God's existence
The distinction between natural and divine law
questions.explanation: Spinoza's 'Deus sive Natura' expresses his pantheistic view that God and Nature are identical — one infinite, self-causing substance.

questions.text: What is the 'naturalistic fallacy', as described by G.E. Moore?
options: Assuming nature is always morally good
The error of deriving moral values from natural facts
questions.explanation: G.E. Moore's naturalistic fallacy is the error of defining moral terms like 'good' using natural properties like pleasure or evolutionary fitness.

questions.text: What is 'compatibilism' in the free will debate?
options: The view that free will and determinism are mutually exclusive
The position that free will and determinism can both be true simultaneously
The belief that only some humans have free will
The rejection of both free will and determinism
questions.explanation: Compatibilism holds that free will and determinism are not mutually exclusive; one can act freely even in a determined universe.

questions.text: What is the 'Sapir-Whorf hypothesis' in philosophy of language?
options: The idea that language evolved from animal communication
The hypothesis that the language we speak shapes or determines our thoughts
The theory that all languages share a universal grammar
The claim that meaning is purely social
questions.explanation: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis holds that the language one speaks influences or determines how one perceives and thinks about the world.

questions.text: What is Heidegger's concept of 'Dasein'?
options: The mode of human being as 'being-in-the-world,' characterized by temporal existence
questions.explanation: Heidegger's 'Dasein' (literally 'being-there') refers to the particular kind of being humans have — always situated, temporal, and world-engaged.

questions.text: What does Wittgenstein mean by 'language games' in his later philosophy?
options: That words gain meaning through their use in specific social practices and contexts
That philosophy is a form of wordplay
That all language is ultimately meaningless
questions.explanation: Wittgenstein's 'language games' concept holds that words derive meaning from their use within particular social practices and forms of life.
